aliases: Traditional Sour Plum Drink with Osmanthus

english: Traditional Sour Plum Drink with Osmanthus

region_of_origin: China (Beijing / Northern China tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for osmanthus plum juice (suanmei tang). FOODON_03311737 (fruit juice beverage) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fruit and vegetable juices and nectars
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Fruit and vegetable juices and nectars) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for osmanthus-infused sour plum beverage.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226465004 (Fruit juice) used as closest parent. No specific SNOMED CT code exists for osmanthus plum juice.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 ml (one cup or one bottle), as typically sold at Chinese beverage stalls and restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Sour Plum Drink (Suanmei Tang) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference values for sweetened fruit-based herbal beverage and published compositional data for suanmei tang
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; supplemented by peer-reviewed compositional literature on suanmei tang
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for osmanthus plum juice (suanmei tang). Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for sweetened fruit-based herbal beverages (plum-based drinks, lightly sweetened herbal teas) and cross-referenced with published compositional analyses of traditional Chinese suanmei tang. The osmanthus variant adds negligible macronutrient difference versus standard suanmei tang. Energy is primarily from dissolved sugars (rock sugar). Protein and fat are effectively zero. Sodium is low. Values represent a typical commercial preparation at approximately 8–10 g sugar per 100 ml.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened fruit-based beverages — analogous estimate based on lightly sweetened plum/fruit drinks; reference: University of Sydney GI Database entries for fruit juice beverages and lightly sweetened herbal drinks
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for osmanthus plum juice (suanmei tang) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on lightly sweetened fruit-based beverages and plum juice entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which typically range from 40–55 for beverages with moderate dissolved sugar content and organic acids. The presence of organic acids (malic, tartaric) from sour plum and hawthorn is known to attenuate glycemic response, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 28.5) / 100 = 12.8 (moderate GL for a 300 g serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~5 mg per 100 ml; ~15 mg per 300 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Osmanthus plum juice is very low in sodium (~15 mg per standard 300 ml serving) and poses minimal concern for hypertension; however, individuals should be mindful of total sugar intake, as high sugar consumption can indirectly contribute to weight gain and elevated blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (~27 g per 300 ml serving) from added rock sugar; low GI (~45) due to organic acids
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a low glycemic index (~45) attributable to organic acids in sour plum and hawthorn, a standard 300 ml serving delivers approximately 27 g of sugar, resulting in a moderate glycemic load (~12.8); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and account for this beverage within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and saturated fat; low sodium; antioxidant compounds from osmanthus and hawthorn
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Osmanthus plum juice contains negligible fat and saturated fat and is low in sodium, making it broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet; hawthorn (shan zha) has been associated with mild cardioprotective effects in traditional Chinese medicine, though clinical evidence remains limited and the beverage's sugar content should be moderated in individuals with metabolic cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Traditional-Style Osmanthus Plum Juice (桂花酸梅汤, Guìhuā Suānméi Tāng) is a classic Chinese herbal beverage with deep roots in Northern Chinese culinary tradition, particularly associated with Beijing street food culture. It is a fragrant variation of the well-known sour plum drink (suanmei tang), distinguished by the addition of dried osmanthus flowers (桂花, guìhuā), which impart a delicate floral aroma and subtle sweetness. The base drink is prepared by simmering dried sour plums (wu mei, 乌梅), hawthorn berries (shan zha, 山楂), licorice root (gan cao, 甘草), and rock sugar in water, then steeping dried osmanthus flowers into the cooled liquid. The resulting beverage is typically served chilled and has a pleasantly tart, lightly sweet, and floral flavour profile with a thin, watery consistency. In Singapore, it is sold at Chinese beverage stalls, traditional herbal drink shops, and Chinese restaurants such as Lan Ting Xu. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-fat beverage with energy derived almost entirely from dissolved sugars (~38 kcal per 100 ml). It has a low glycemic index (~45) due to the organic acids present in sour plum and hawthorn, which attenuate postprandial glucose response. Sodium content is very low (~5 mg per 100 ml). The beverage is suitable for vegetarians and vegans and is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, it is broadly compatible with hypertension and cardiovascular disease management due to its low sodium and fat content, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate intake given the moderate glycemic load per serving (~12.8 for 300 ml). Hawthorn berries have been studied for mild cardioprotective and digestive properties in traditional Chinese medicine contexts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in osmanthus plum juice?
Is sour plum drink good for diabetics?

What are the ingredients in traditional sour plum drink?
Can I drink osmanthus plum juice if I have hypertension?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for osmanthus plum juice (suanmei tang) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable sweetened fruit-based herbal beverages and cross-referenced with published compositional literature on suanmei tang. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar lightly sweetened fruit-based beverages. Confidence is medium rather than low because the beverage composition is well-characterised in the literature and the estimation basis is defensible.
notes: 1. Fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — osmanthus plum juice is an aqueous sugar-based beverage with no fat-containing ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central analogous beverage data. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are all 0.0 for the same reason. 2. Protein_g is 0.1 (effectively trace): the beverage contains negligible protein from plant-derived steeping ingredients; value is trace. 3. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source database entries for this beverage type. Vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 4. GI set to 45 (analogous estimate, low category) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for lightly sweetened fruit-based beverages; organic acids in sour plum and hawthorn support low GI classification. 5. Xinyuanzhai is a brand/restaurant name associated with this beverage in Singapore; it has been noted in aliases for retrieval purposes. 6. This record is served by Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles at Lazada One, Singapore (field survey source). 7. glycemic_index set to 45 (analogous estimate); gi_category low per University of Sydney GI Database convention for lightly sweetened fruit-based herbal beverages with organic acid content.
